header.subtitle: Module 4: Claude Hooks & Automated Trust
sec.loop.intro: The goal of Claude Hooks is to create a self-validating system. The AI doesn't just write the code. It is forced to test and fix it before delivering the final result. Click on the stages to understand the flow.
sec.loop.node1.title: Write
sec.loop.node1.desc: AI generates the initial code based on the prompt.
sec.loop.node2.title: Lint & Style
sec.loop.node2.desc: Automatic hook runs the project's Linter.
sec.loop.node3.title: Tests
sec.loop.node3.desc: Hook runs the unit test suite.
sec.loop.node4.title: Fix
sec.loop.node4.desc: If it fails, the AI reads the error and fixes it on its own.
sec.loop.loopback: If the fix fails, the cycle restarts from Lint, until all hooks return exit 0
sec.loop.placeholder: Click on a stage to see automation details.
sec.hooks.intro: Hooks aren't just for file writing. Claude Code exposes 4 lifecycle events of the agent that you can intercept. Each one serves a different purpose.
sec.hooks.pre.desc: Runs before any tool is executed by the agent. Can block or modify the action before it happens.
sec.hooks.pre.li.1: • Validate if the file is in the allowlist
sec.hooks.pre.li.3: • Log an audit trail of every agent action
sec.hooks.post.desc: Runs after a tool completes. Receives the full output of the operation. This is the most commonly used hook for lint and tests.
sec.hooks.stop.desc: Runs when Claude finishes the complete response to the user. Ideal for wrap-up actions and notifications.
sec.hooks.stop.li.1: • Notify via Slack when a task finishes
sec.hooks.stop.li.2: • Generate a report of changes made
sec.hooks.notif.desc: Runs when Claude emits an internal alert or notification, such as permission warnings or long-running operation status.
sec.hooks.notif.li.1: • Customize permission alerts for the team
sec.hooks.matcher.desc: Each hook uses a matcher to filter which tool it should fire on. Without a matcher, it fires on all tools.
sec.hooks.matcher.write: Only fires when the agent creates a new file.
sec.config.intro: The configuration lives in ~/.claude/settings.json (global) or .claude/settings.json (per project). This file connects the agent to your development environment.
sec.config.envVars.toolName: Name of the tool that triggered the hook
sec.config.envVars.sessionId: Unique ID of the current session
sec.config.whyHooks.title: Why Hooks instead of CI/CD?
sec.config.whyHooks.desc: CI/CD runs after the push. The Hook runs during code generation. The AI receives the terminal error in real time and doesn't deliver the result until the command returns success.
sec.config.globalVsProject.title: Global vs. Per Project
sec.config.globalVsProject.desc: Hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json apply to all projects. Hooks in .claude/settings.json are repository-specific and can be versioned with the team.
sec.limits.intro: Hooks are powerful, but misconfigured they can freeze your workflow or create false positives. Know the risks before putting them in production.
sec.limits.dangers.loop.title: Infinite Loop
sec.limits.dangers.loop.desc: If the hook always returns failure (e.g., lint with an impossible-to-satisfy rule), Claude gets stuck in a loop trying to fix it indefinitely. Always test the hook manually before enabling it.
sec.limits.dangers.loop.tip: ✓ Set a maximum timeout on the command: timeout 30 npm run lint
sec.limits.dangers.security.title: Security Permissions
sec.limits.dangers.security.desc: Hooks execute with your system permissions. A poorly written or injected hook can execute arbitrary commands. Never copy hook configurations from unknown sources without reviewing them.
sec.limits.dangers.security.tip: ✓ Prefer hooks with the minimum required scope
sec.limits.warnings.perf.title: Performance in large projects
sec.limits.warnings.perf.desc: Running the entire test suite on every PostToolUse in a monorepo with 10k tests will freeze your workflow. Use the matcher to limit scope and filter only the tests related to the modified file.
sec.limits.warnings.external.title: External dependencies
sec.limits.warnings.external.desc: Hooks that call external APIs, databases, or network services can fail for reasons beyond your control (timeout, no internet). This will block Claude even if the code is correct. Isolate code validation hooks from integration hooks.
sec.limits.warnings.judgment.title: AI does not replace human judgment
sec.limits.warnings.judgment.desc: Hooks validate what is machine-verifiable: syntax, types, coverage. They don't detect wrong business logic, edge cases not covered by tests, or bad architectural decisions. AI-reviewed code still needs a human eye, just at a higher level.
sec.limits.practices.li.1: Start with a single simple hook. Add complexity gradually.
sec.limits.practices.li.2: Always use timeout on commands that can hang.
sec.limits.practices.li.3: Version the .claude/settings.json in the project repository.
sec.limits.practices.li.4: Document in the README what each hook does and why it exists.
sec.metrics.intro: The time savings don't come from writing, but from review. When the AI self-corrects, human time is freed for architectural decisions.
sec.metrics.before.title: Manual Review (Before)
sec.metrics.before.desc: "Missing a semicolon", "That variable name is bad", "Forgot to handle the catch".
sec.metrics.after.title: Review with Hooks (After)
sec.metrics.after.desc: "Does this abstraction make sense for the business?", "Is the API design scalable?".
sec.metrics.footnote: Data simulates the effort transition after implementing hooks. Manual review time drops progressively as confidence in the test suite increases.
sec.review.intro: What does your day-to-day look like after unclogging the bottleneck? Your seniority is now applied where AI still fails: Intentionality and Business Logic.
sec.review.before.li.3: Too fatigued for what really matters.
sec.review.after.li.1: Linter and tests already passed via Hooks.
sec.review.after.li.3: Focusing on Architecture and Business Logic.
sec.review.mantra.label: The Part 4 Mantra:
sec.review.mantra.text: "The machine validates what is verifiable. You judge what is intentional."
sec.nextsteps.intro: You don't need to configure everything at once. The safest path is to start simple, validate, and expand. Here's a 4-step roadmap.
sec.nextsteps.step1.title: Create the configuration file
sec.nextsteps.step1.desc: Create or edit your project's hooks file:
sec.nextsteps.step2.title: Add a lint hook as a first test
sec.nextsteps.step2.desc: Start with lint only: it's fast, safe, and gives immediate feedback:
sec.nextsteps.step3.title: Validate on a real task
sec.nextsteps.step3.desc: Ask Claude to create or modify a file and watch the hook fire in the terminal. Confirm that:
sec.nextsteps.step3.li.1: Lint ran automatically after the write
sec.nextsteps.step3.li.2: A lint error forced Claude to retry
sec.nextsteps.step3.li.3: The final delivered result was already clean
sec.nextsteps.step4.title: Expand with tests and version with the team
sec.nextsteps.step4.desc: Once lint is working well, add tests. Then commit .claude/settings.json so the whole team benefits:

nodeData.1.title: Write
nodeData.1.body: The AI proposes code based on your prompt. Without hooks, this would be the end of the line, and you'd have to manually review every syntax and style detail.
nodeData.2.title: Lint & Style
nodeData.2.body: The PostToolUse hook with matcher Write|Edit fires as soon as the file is saved and runs Prettier/ESLint. If there's an error, the AI receives the full terminal log and automatically attempts a new fix.
nodeData.3.title: Tests
nodeData.3.body: The hook runs vitest run or jest on the affected files. The AI "feels" in real time if it broke something in the existing logic and includes the stack trace in the next fix attempt.
nodeData.4.title: Autonomous Fix
nodeData.4.body: When lint or tests fail, Claude reads the terminal output, understands the error, and rewrites the problematic section. The cycle repeats until all hooks return code 0 (success). Only then is the result delivered to you.
